_comment: Deterministic rotation. Ordered to interleave categories: knee -> hip -> balance -> upper, repeating. Each segment is either timed ({label, seconds}) or rep-based ({label, reps}). work_friendly=true means you can keep working through it.

exercises.name: Single-leg mini squats
exercises.cue: Stand on one leg, fingertips on the desk for balance. Let the other foot hover just off the floor, extended slightly forward as a counterbalance — don't let it touch down or take weight. Do slow quarter-squats: bend the standing knee to about 45° (a shallow dip, shin near-vertical), then rise. Feel the muscle just above and inside the kneecap (VMO) work, and keep the knee tracking over the middle toes — don't let it cave inward.
exercises.note: Targets VMO + glute-medius control — the muscles that stabilize the kneecap for cycling.

exercises.name: Standing 'stealth' hip-flexor stretch
exercises.cue: Split stance, back foot pointing forward; come up onto the BALL of the back foot (heel lifted) so the stretch doesn't get eaten by the calf. The key: tuck your tailbone under / flatten your lower back (posterior tilt) and squeeze the back-side glute HARD — if you feel it in the quad, you're not tucked enough. Stay tall (don't lean from the waist) and drive forward by bending the FRONT knee until you feel it in the FRONT of the back hip. Ease off before it's sharp.
exercises.note: Pelvic tuck is everything — arched back = the psoas stays slack and you stretch nothing useful.

exercises.name: PVC shoulder pass-throughs (dislocates)
exercises.cue: Wide overhand grip on the pipe. Keep arms straight and slowly raise it overhead and behind you, then back to the front. Go only as wide as you need to keep the elbows locked; narrow the grip over time as shoulders open up.
exercises.note: Thoracic + shoulder mobility to counter the cycling/desk hunch.

exercises.name: Ulnar nerve glide (nerve floss)
exercises.cue: For the numb hand: start with the arm straight out to the side, palm up. Bend the elbow and TWIST the forearm so the palm rotates all the way around toward your face; make an 'OK' ring with thumb + index and bring it up around your eye like goggles, so the PALM ends up flat against your temple with the other three fingers pointing down your cheek. You should feel a GENTLE pull or tingle into the ring + pinky finger — ease off before it's painful. Slowly untwist back out and repeat.
exercises.note: For cyclist's/handlebar palsy — flosses the ulnar nerve so it glides instead of catching. Keep it gentle; nerve work should never be forced. If numbness persists for hours/days, weakens grip, or wastes the thumb pad, see a hand specialist.

exercises.name: Wall sit
exercises.cue: Back flat against the wall, feet shoulder-width and a step out from the wall. Slide down until knees are ~90° (shins vertical). Hold. Weight through the heels.
exercises.note: Isometric quad/VMO builder. Shorten the hold if the knees complain.

exercises.name: Standing hip CARs (controlled circles)
exercises.cue: Stand on one leg (hold the desk). Lift the other knee to hip height and draw the biggest slow circle you can with the knee. Move only at the hip; keep the rest of you still.
exercises.note: Actively drives end-range hip rotation that cycling never asks for.

exercises.name: PVC overhead reach + side bend
exercises.cue: Hold the pipe overhead, hands wide, arms straight. Reach tall, then bend slowly to one side feeling the stretch down the ribs and lat. Hold, return, then the other side.
exercises.note: Opens the lats and side-body that shorten in the cycling/desk fold.

exercises.name: Gyro ball forearm spin
exercises.cue: Spin the gyro ball up to a strong, steady buzz before the timer starts, then keep it going with small wrist circles. Grip firm but not white-knuckled. Ease off on the affected side if it aggravates the numbness.
exercises.note: Builds forearm + grip endurance so you can ride with a relaxed grip and less sustained pressure on the ulnar nerve — the strengthening complement to the nerve glide.

exercises.name: Externally-rotated half squats
exercises.cue: Feet shoulder-width, toes and knees turned outward ~30°. Squat halfway down, then rise slowly, deliberately driving the knees outward and feeling the inner-quad (VMO) pull you up.
exercises.note: The turned-out stance biases load toward the VMO.

exercises.name: Standing figure-4 hip stretch
exercises.cue: Hold the desk. Cross one ankle over the opposite thigh just above the knee, then sit the hips back into a shallow one-leg squat until you feel the stretch in the outer hip/glute. Keep the crossed foot flexed.
exercises.note: Targets the glute-med/piriformis — the outer-hip stabilizers cyclists neglect.

exercises.name: Balance-board calf stretch
exercises.cue: Heels on the floor, place the balls/arches of both feet on the near edge of the board and let it tip back so your toes ride higher than your heels. That forefoot-up position stretches both calves and Achilles at once. Legs straight, ease your weight in, hold still — no bouncing. Brace on the desk if you like.
exercises.note: Forefoot-elevated dorsiflexion (toes higher than heels) — the classic calf/Achilles stretch, both feet at once. Good for cyclists' tight calves; keep it gentle.

exercises.name: PVC thoracic rotations
exercises.cue: Rest the pipe across the back of your shoulders, hands draped over it. Stand tall, brace the core, and rotate your upper back slowly left and right — turn from the mid-back, keep the hips facing forward.
exercises.note: Restores the spinal rotation that hours in one posture erase.
